title: Sourcing and settling point-of-lay hens
subtitle: What to look for when buying, and the first week that sets the pattern.
excerpt: Point-of-lay hens are the standard starting point for a backyard flock: old enough to lay within weeks, young enough to give several seasons. What matters is finding a reputable source, knowing what a healthy pullet looks like, and managing the first week so the birds associate the coop with home before they start roaming.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Laying Hens, 2018. Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive. University of Wisconsin Extension, Backyard Poultry Series.

glossaryTerms.definition: A pullet at approximately 16-20 weeks old that is about to begin laying. POL is a commercial term; the exact age varies by breed. Heavy breeds may not lay until 24 weeks; light Mediterranean breeds can start at 16 weeks.
A female chicken in her first laying year, from hatch until approximately 12 months old. After 12 months she is called a hen.

A period of isolation for newly acquired stock before introducing them to an existing flock, allowing time to observe for signs of disease or parasites. Standard poultry quarantine is 2-4 weeks in a separate housing.

content.text: hens is the most common entry point for a backyard poultry flock. The birds are old enough to be robust and are weeks or days from laying their first eggs. Done well, sourcing from a reputable breeder or farm gives you a healthy, vaccinated flock. Done carelessly, it introduces disease into a setup that had none.

The best source is a small, specialist breeding farm that hatches its own stock, vaccinates against Marek's disease as standard, and allows farm visits. Avoid markets and auctions for a first flock; the disease risk from mixing birds of unknown origin is high. Online classified ads for surplus hens are variable; if buying from a private keeper, ask when the birds last had a worm egg count and whether they have been treated for red mite.
What a healthy pullet looks like

at point of lay stands upright, holds her tail raised, and is alert to movement and sound. Her comb is beginning to redden (pale pink in lighter breeds at 16 weeks, deepening as laying approaches). Her vent is moist and pink. Her feathers are smooth and complete. She should move freely without favouring a leg, and her eyes should be clear and bright. Reject a bird that squints, sits hunched, has discharge around the nostrils, or has bald patches suggesting red mite or heavy parasitism.

Transfer birds in ventilated boxes, preferably at dusk. Place them directly into the closed coop with food and water already inside. Keep the pop-hole closed for the first 24 hours so they associate the coop interior with safety before they go into the run. On day two, open the pop-hole mid-morning, not at dawn. Birds that come out into the run and then return to the coop at dusk on their own have imprinted on the housing. Birds that do not return before dark need guiding in by hand for the first two or three evenings.
If adding new pullets to an existing flock, observe the

of at least 2 weeks in separate housing before mixing. When integrating, allow the birds to see each other through wire before sharing a run. Provide multiple feeding and drinking points so lower-ranking birds are not excluded from resources by established flock members.

Expect the first egg within 2-6 weeks of collection for most hybrid layer pullets at point of lay. Pure breeds take longer. The first eggs from any pullet are smaller than her eventual settled size; this is normal and rights itself within a few weeks.
